Title: High-affinity iron uptake is required for optimal Epichloë festucae colonization of Lolium perenne and seed transmission

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential micronutrient for fungal growth, iron homeostasis, symbiotic colonization, and seed transmission
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene disruption (ΔfetC); double mutant construction (ΔfetC/ΔsidA); gene complementation (ΔfetC/fetC); growth assays under iron gradients; LC-MS siderophore measurements; microscopy; qPCR biomass assays; tiller and seed transmission assays
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: fetC is required for optimal reductive iron assimilation, normal hyphal growth, full endophyte biomass, and efficient tiller/seed transmission; deletion causes mild iron-deficiency growth defects and siderophore hyperaccumulation, while combined deletion with sidA causes severe defects
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. fetC encodes the multicopper ferroxidase component of reductive iron assimilation in E. festucae. 2. ΔfetC mutants grow slightly slower than WT under iron deficiency and hyperaccumulate ferriepichloënin A at low iron and ferricrocin plus ferriepichloënin A at higher iron. 3. Loss of fetC reduces in planta biomass and causes hyperbranched hyphae; combined loss with sidA strongly reduces host colonization and vertical transmission.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Part of a gene cluster with ftrA; likely coexpressed/divergently arranged
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Iron chelation (100 μM BPS); 0-1000 μM FeCl3; exogenous ferriepichloënin A; in planta growth; hydroponic high- and low-iron conditions
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS siderophore profiling; qPCR biomass quantification; gene expression assay for ftrA mentioned; raw data in Table S3
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating fetC or fungal iron uptake could alter endophyte colonization and transmission in forage systems; useful for controlling endophyte fitness rather than enhancing host nutrient content
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disruption causes reduced fungal growth under low iron, altered hyphal morphology, reduced reproductive tiller percentage, and lower endophyte transmission, especially in combination with sidA
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: fetC forms a cluster with ftrA; deletion design removed ~500 bp intergenic promoter region and may have affected ftrA expression; although ferroxidase, it was the main experimental subject for iron assimilation and is categorized here as Common_Gene per task taxonomy focusing non-TF/noncanonical nutrient-handling role

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Siderophore biosynthesis / high-affinity iron uptake pathway
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Hydroxamate siderophore biosynthesis for ferricrocin and epichloënin A/ferriepichloënin A production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: High-affinity iron chelation, uptake, intracellular sequestration, oxidative stress protection, and support of fungal symbiosis and transmission
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Ferricrocin in mycelia (intracellular); ferriepichloënin A in mycelia and culture supernatant (dual intracellular/extracellular)
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly required for biosynthesis of the target siderophores
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene disruption (ΔsidA); double mutant construction (ΔfetC/ΔsidA); gene complementation (ΔsidA/sidA); LC-MS siderophore profiling; fungal growth assays under variable iron; in planta colonization and transmission assays
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of sidA abolished siderophore production, severely reduced growth under iron deficiency, altered hyphal morphology, reduced endophyte biomass, and impaired transmission especially when combined with fetC deletion
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. sidA encodes a siderophore biosynthesis enzyme required for production of ferricrocin and ferriepichloënin A. 2. ΔsidA mutants showed no detectable siderophore production and strong iron-deficiency growth defects. 3. Combined loss of sidA and fetC caused the strongest colonization and seed-transmission defects, demonstrating complementary roles of siderophore-mediated uptake and RIA.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Iron chelation (100 μM BPS); 0-1000 μM FeCl3; exogenous ferriepichloënin A; in planta growth; hydroponic high- and low-iron conditions
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step in siderophore biosynthesis; committed step
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS siderophore profiling; qPCR biomass quantification; raw data in Table S3
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Targeting siderophore biosynthesis genes such as sidA could reduce endophyte persistence and vertical transmission; useful for manipulating fungal fitness rather than crop nutrient biofortification
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of sidA causes severe fungal growth defects, oxidative stress sensitivity, abnormal hyphae, reduced colonization, and reduced reproductive tiller percentage in infected plants
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Complementation was via ectopic integration and may not fully restore native function; sidA is part of a divergently coexpressed genomic context
